--- title: "Overall Effect Size Calculation & Synthesis" subtitle: "Meta-Analytic Update of Mate-Choice Copying (MCC)" author: "Eduardo S. A. Santos & Collaborators" date: today format: html: toc: true toc-depth: 3 theme: cosmo self-contained: true --- ## Introduction & Project Update This protocol implements the overall effect size synthesis for the updated Mate-Choice Copying (MCC) meta-analysis. Specifically, we integrate: 1. **The New Extractions:** Our newly extracted and verified dataset containing recent trials. 2. **Original Continuous Dataset (@daviesMetaanalysisFactorsInfluencing2020):** Standardised mean differences (Hedges' $d$) analyzing continuous metrics. 3. **Original Binary Dataset (@jonesMechanismsSocialInfluence2019):** Log Odds Ratio ($\ln\text{OR}$) analyzing binary choices. The R script aligns and synthesizes these three sources into unified datasets, allowing comparison of meta-analytic summaries across three key subsets: * **New Extractions Only:** Eduardo's new dataset. * **Original Only:** Legacy data from the original publications. * **Combined Dataset:** A complete synthesis of old and new trials to estimate the updated global effect sizes. --- ## Data Loading and Alignment We read the three datasets, perform data type conversions, extract species epithets for taxonomic control, and align columns into unified Hedges' $g$ and Odds Ratio datasets. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # 1. Load Datasets # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # Paths to legacy and new datasets path_new <- here::here("data", "1_effect_size_calculation_pipeline", "Data Extraction Mate Choice Meta Analysis.xlsx") path_davies <- here::here("data", "original_meta_analyses_datasets", "Davies_et_al_2020_Final_data.xlsx") path_jones <- here::here("data", "original_meta_analyses_datasets", "Jones_DuVal_2019_data.CSV") # A. Read new extraction dataset (EduardoExtraction sheet) db_new_raw <- readxl::read_excel(path_new, sheet = "EduardoExtraction") # B. Read Davies et al. 2020 (Original continuous dataset) db_davies_raw <- readxl::read_excel(path_davies, sheet = "Data") # C. Read Jones & DuVal 2019 (Original binary dataset) db_jones_raw <- readr::read_csv(path_jones) # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # 1b. Species-name reconciliation + phylogenetic correlation matrices # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # The phylogenetic correlation matrices (built in scripts/2_phylogeny/ # build_phylogeny.R) are indexed by FULL species binomials. The mappings below # reproduce that script's reconciliation so the `species_phylo` grouping in the # effect-size data matches the tree tip labels (and matrix dimnames) exactly. recode_new <- c("Taeniopygia guttata castanotis" = "Taeniopygia guttata") recode_davies <- c("Schizocosa" = "Schizocosa ocreata") # Jones & DuVal store only the species EPITHET; rebuild full binomials from # Family + epithet (identical to build_phylogeny.R). jones_binomial <- tibble::tribble( ~family, ~epithet, ~species_phylo, "Adrianichthyidae", "latipes", "Oryzias latipes", "Blenniidae", "nitidus", "Rhabdoblennius nitidus", "Gasterosteidae", "aculeatus", "Gasterosteus aculeatus", "Gobiidae", "minutu", "Pomatoschistus minutus", "Percidae", "olmstedi", "Etheostoma olmstedi", "Poeciliidae", "latipinna", "Poecilia latipinna", "Poeciliidae", "nigrofasciata", "Limia nigrofasciata", "Poeciliidae", "perugiae", "Limia perugiae", "Poeciliidae", "reticulata", "Poecilia reticulata", "Pomacentridae", "leucogaster", "Amblyglyphidodon leucogaster", "Lycosidae", "ocreata", "Schizocosa ocreata", "Estrildidae", "guttata", "Taeniopygia guttata", "Icteridae", "ater", "Molothrus ater", "Muscicapidae", "hypoleuca", "Ficedula hypoleuca", "Drosophilidae", "melanogaster", "Drosophila melanogaster", "Drosophilidae", "serrata", "Drosophila serrata", "Ocypodidae", "mjoebergi", "Uca mjoebergi" ) # Phylogenetic correlation matrices (full binomials as dimnames). Loaded # defensively: if the phylogeny build has not been run, these are NULL and the # models gracefully fall back to a non-phylogenetic species random effect only. read_phylo <- function(f) { p <- here::here("outputs", "2_phylogeny", f) if (file.exists(p)) readRDS(p) else NULL } phylo_cor_new <- read_phylo("phylo_cor_new_extraction.rds") # new-extraction tree phylo_cor_hedges <- read_phylo("phylo_cor_hedges_model.rds") # combined Hedges' g species phylo_cor_or <- read_phylo("phylo_cor_or_model.rds") # combined log OR species # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # 2. Process and Filter New Extraction # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- db_new <- db_new_raw %>% filter(is.na(RemoveFromDataset) | RemoveFromDataset != "Yes") %>% mutate( effectSizeCalculatedHedgesD = as.numeric(effectSizeCalculatedHedgesD), effectSizeCalculatedHedgesDVariance = as.numeric(effectSizeCalculatedHedgesDVariance), effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatio = as.numeric(effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatio), effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatioVariance = as.numeric(effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatioVariance), logOddsRatio = log(effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatio), logOddsRatioVariance = effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatioVariance ) # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # 3. Align Datasets for Hedges' g (Continuous SMD Analysis) # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # A. Hedges' g from New Extraction hedges_new <- db_new %>% filter(!is.na(effectSizeCalculatedHedgesD), !is.na(effectSizeCalculatedHedgesDVariance)) %>% select( yi = effectSizeCalculatedHedgesD, vi = effectSizeCalculatedHedgesDVariance, identifierStudyId, identifierEffectSizeID, taxonomySpecies ) %>% mutate( identifierStudyId = as.character(identifierStudyId), identifierEffectSizeID = as.character(identifierEffectSizeID), # Full binomial for the phylogeny (matches tree tip labels / matrix dimnames) species_phylo = dplyr::recode(str_squish(as.character(taxonomySpecies)), !!!recode_new), # Extract species epithet (last word) and make lowercase for cross-dataset mapping taxonomySpecies = str_to_lower(word(as.character(taxonomySpecies), -1)), Dataset_Source = "New Extraction (Santos)" ) # B. Hedges' g from Davies et al. 2020 (Original) hedges_davies <- db_davies_raw %>% mutate( Hedges_d_directional = as.numeric(Hedges_d_directional), Variance = as.numeric(Variance) ) %>% filter(!is.na(Hedges_d_directional), !is.na(Variance), Variance > 0) %>% select( yi = Hedges_d_directional, vi = Variance, identifierStudyId = Author, identifierEffectSizeID = Effect_size_no, taxonomySpecies = Species_latin ) %>% mutate( identifierStudyId = as.character(identifierStudyId), identifierEffectSizeID = as.character(identifierEffectSizeID), # Full binomial for the phylogeny (matches tree tip labels / matrix dimnames) species_phylo = dplyr::recode(str_squish(as.character(taxonomySpecies)), !!!recode_davies), # Extract species epithet (last word) and make lowercase taxonomySpecies = str_to_lower(word(as.character(taxonomySpecies), -1)), Dataset_Source = "Original (Davies 2020)" ) # C. Unified Hedges' g Dataset db_hedges <- bind_rows(hedges_new, hedges_davies) %>% mutate(across(c(identifierStudyId, taxonomySpecies, species_phylo, Dataset_Source), as.factor)) # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # 4. Align Datasets for Odds Ratio (Binary Analysis) # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # A. Log Odds Ratio from New Extraction or_new <- db_new %>% filter(!is.na(logOddsRatio), !is.na(logOddsRatioVariance)) %>% select( yi = logOddsRatio, vi = logOddsRatioVariance, identifierStudyId, identifierEffectSizeID, taxonomySpecies ) %>% mutate( identifierStudyId = as.character(identifierStudyId), identifierEffectSizeID = as.character(identifierEffectSizeID), # Full binomial for the phylogeny (matches tree tip labels / matrix dimnames) species_phylo = dplyr::recode(str_squish(as.character(taxonomySpecies)), !!!recode_new), taxonomySpecies = str_to_lower(word(as.character(taxonomySpecies), -1)), Dataset_Source = "New Extraction (Santos)" ) # B. Log Odds Ratio from Jones & DuVal 2019 (Original) or_jones <- db_jones_raw %>% filter(!is.na(lnOR), !is.na(VlnOR), VlnOR > 0) %>% mutate( family = str_squish(as.character(Family)), epithet = str_squish(as.character(Species)) ) %>% # Rebuild full binomial (species_phylo) from Family + epithet left_join(jones_binomial, by = c("family", "epithet")) %>% select( yi = lnOR, vi = VlnOR, identifierStudyId = study, identifierEffectSizeID = study_Exp, taxonomySpecies = Species, species_phylo ) %>% mutate( identifierStudyId = as.character(identifierStudyId), identifierEffectSizeID = as.character(identifierEffectSizeID), taxonomySpecies = str_to_lower(as.character(taxonomySpecies)), Dataset_Source = "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)" ) # Sanity check: every Jones epithet should map to a full binomial if (any(is.na(or_jones$species_phylo))) { warning( "Unmapped Jones epithets (phylogeny): ", paste(unique(or_jones$taxonomySpecies[is.na(or_jones$species_phylo)]), collapse = ", ") ) } # C. Unified Log Odds Ratio Dataset db_or <- bind_rows(or_new, or_jones) %>% mutate(across(c(identifierStudyId, taxonomySpecies, species_phylo, Dataset_Source), as.factor)) ``` ::: --- ## Study Inclusion & Exclusion {#sec-inclusion} The following tables document which effect sizes from the new extraction are included in the meta-analytic models and which have been removed, with the reason for each exclusion. ### Excluded Effect Sizes ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Included Effect Sizes ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: --- ## Dataset Composition Summary {#sec-composition} Before inspecting the effect size distributions, we summarize how the aligned datasets are composed. The first table reports the number of studies (`k`) and effect sizes contributed by each original source and by our new extraction. The second table summarizes the number of effect sizes per species, ordered from most to least common. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Combine both aligned effect-size datasets, tagging the effect-size class. # Note: the New Extraction (Santos) contributes to BOTH the Hedges' g and the # Odds Ratio datasets; n_distinct() on study IDs therefore avoids double-counting # studies while total effect sizes are summed across classes. db_summary_all <- bind_rows( db_hedges %>% mutate(EffectType = "Hedges' g"), db_or %>% mutate(EffectType = "Log Odds Ratio") ) %>% mutate(across(c(identifierStudyId, taxonomySpecies, Dataset_Source, EffectType), as.character)) ``` ::: ### Studies & Effect Sizes by Source ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Effect Sizes per Species ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: In total, the aligned data comprise 399 effect sizes from 120 studies across 36 species. The most-studied species is *Melanogaster* (113 effect sizes from 22 studies). --- ## Effect Size Distribution (Density Plots) {#sec-density} Before fitting the multi-level models, we inspect the distribution of our aligned effect sizes across the different subsets (New Extraction, Original Only, and Combined). The density plots below use the same split logic as the orchard plots in the result section, facet-wrapping by subset while coloring the distribution curves by their actual dataset source to check for shifts or outliers. ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Hedges' $g$ (Continuous SMD) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Prepare density data for Hedges' g db_hedges_density <- bind_rows( db_hedges %>% mutate(Group = as.character(Dataset_Source)), db_hedges %>% mutate(Group = "Combined") ) %>% mutate(Group = factor(Group, levels = c("New Extraction (Santos)", "Original (Davies 2020)", "Combined"))) # Plot densities p_hedges_dens <- ggplot(db_hedges_density, aes(x = yi, fill = Dataset_Source, color = Dataset_Source)) + geom_density(alpha = 0.4, linewidth = 0.8) + geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linewidth = 1.0, linetype = "solid", color = "black") + geom_rug(alpha = 0.6) + facet_wrap(~Group, ncol = 1) + scale_fill_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Davies 2020)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") + scale_color_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Davies 2020)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") + labs( title = "Distribution of Hedges' g Effect Sizes", x = "Hedges' g", y = "Density" ) + theme_minimal(base_size = 12) + theme( legend.position = "bottom", strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11), plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5) ) print(p_hedges_dens) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](overall_effect_files/figure-html/hedges_density_plots-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ### Odds Ratio (Binary outcomes) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Prepare density data for Odds Ratio (lnOR) db_or_density <- bind_rows( db_or %>% mutate(Group = as.character(Dataset_Source)), db_or %>% mutate(Group = "Combined") ) %>% mutate(Group = factor(Group, levels = c("New Extraction (Santos)", "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)", "Combined"))) # Plot densities p_or_dens <- ggplot(db_or_density, aes(x = yi, fill = Dataset_Source, color = Dataset_Source)) + geom_density(alpha = 0.4, linewidth = 0.8) + geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linewidth = 1.0, linetype = "solid", color = "black") + geom_rug(alpha = 0.6) + facet_wrap(~Group, ncol = 1) + scale_fill_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") + scale_color_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") + labs( title = "Distribution of Log Odds Ratio (lnOR) Effect Sizes", x = "Log Odds Ratio (lnOR)", y = "Density" ) + theme_minimal(base_size = 12) + theme( legend.position = "bottom", strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11), plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5) ) print(p_or_dens) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](overall_effect_files/figure-html/or_density_plots-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ::: --- ## Model Fitting Function This helper function handles the variance–covariance (VCV) matrix construction and fits a multilevel random-effects model using `rma.mv()` in `metafor`. * **Variance–Covariance:** We construct a VCV matrix via `vcalc()` to handle dependencies among multiple effect sizes within the same study (assuming $r = 0.5$ for nested measurements). * **Random intercepts** are modeled for: * Study (`identifierStudyId`) * Individual trial/effect size (`identifierEffectSizeID`) * Species (`taxonomySpecies`) — the *non-phylogenetic* species effect, capturing species-specific deviations not explained by shared ancestry * Phylogeny (`species_phylo`) — a *phylogenetic* random effect supplied with a species-by-species correlation matrix (`R = list(species_phylo = phylo_cor)`) derived from the Open Tree of Life topology with Grafen branch lengths (see `scripts/2_phylogeny/build_phylogeny.R`). This partitions the among-species variance into a component attributable to phylogenetic relatedness and a residual species component. The phylogenetic term is added to the **uncorrected** models only (for the New, Original, and Combined datasets); the small-study bias-corrected (Egger-type) models retain the original structure. When a phylogenetic correlation matrix is unavailable, the fit gracefully falls back to the non-phylogenetic structure. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} fit_overall_mv <- function(dat, yi = "yi", vi = "vi", cluster_var = "identifierStudyId", obs_var = "identifierEffectSizeID", rho = 0.5, phylo_cor = NULL, phylo_var = "species_phylo") { # Remove rows with missing values dat2 <- dat %>% filter(!is.na(.data[[yi]]), !is.na(.data[[vi]]), .data[[vi]] > 0) if (nrow(dat2) == 0) { message("No complete cases found.") return(NULL) } # 1. Build VCV Matrix VCV <- metafor::vcalc( vi = dat2[[vi]], cluster = dat2[[cluster_var]], obs = dat2[[obs_var]], rho = rho, data = dat2 ) # 2. Assemble random-effects structure. Always include study, effect-size, # and a (non-phylogenetic) species intercept. If a phylogenetic # correlation matrix is supplied AND every species level is present in it, # add a phylogenetic random effect with R = subset-of-correlation-matrix. random_list <- list( ~ 1 | identifierStudyId, ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies ) R_arg <- NULL used_phylo <- FALSE if (!is.null(phylo_cor) && phylo_var %in% colnames(dat2)) { sp <- unique(as.character(dat2[[phylo_var]])) missing_sp <- setdiff(sp, rownames(phylo_cor)) if (length(missing_sp) == 0) { # Subset the correlation matrix to the species actually present so the # dimnames match the grouping levels exactly. Also drop any stale # (unused) factor levels on the grouping column so metafor's level # set matches the R-matrix dimnames. dat2[[phylo_var]] <- factor(as.character(dat2[[phylo_var]])) Csub <- phylo_cor[sp, sp, drop = FALSE] random_list <- c(random_list, list(stats::as.formula(paste0("~ 1 | ", phylo_var)))) R_arg <- stats::setNames(list(Csub), phylo_var) used_phylo <- TRUE } else { warning( "Phylogenetic random effect skipped (species absent from correlation matrix): ", paste(missing_sp, collapse = ", ") ) } } # 3. Fit Multi-Level Random-Effects Model mod <- metafor::rma.mv( yi = dat2[[yi]], V = VCV, random = random_list, R = R_arg, test = "t", method = "REML", sparse = TRUE, data = dat2 ) list(data = dat2, VCV = VCV, model = mod, used_phylo = used_phylo) } # Helper function to fit bias-corrected multilevel models with a continuous moderator (Egger-type) fit_corrected_mv <- function(dat, yi = "yi", vi = "vi", moderator = "vi", cluster_var = "identifierStudyId", obs_var = "identifierEffectSizeID", rho = 0.5) { # Remove rows with missing values dat2 <- dat %>% filter(!is.na(.data[[yi]]), !is.na(.data[[vi]]), .data[[vi]] > 0) if (nrow(dat2) == 0) { message("No complete cases found.") return(NULL) } if (moderator == "se" && !"se" %in% colnames(dat2)) { dat2 <- dat2 %>% mutate(se = sqrt(vi)) } # 1. Build VCV Matrix VCV <- metafor::vcalc( vi = dat2[[vi]], cluster = dat2[[cluster_var]], obs = dat2[[obs_var]], rho = rho, data = dat2 ) # 2. Fit Multi-Level Egger Model using dynamic formula formula_str <- paste("~ 1 +", moderator) mod <- metafor::rma.mv( yi = dat2[[yi]], V = VCV, mods = as.formula(formula_str), random = list( ~ 1 | identifierStudyId, ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies ), test = "t", method = "REML", sparse = TRUE, data = dat2 ) list(data = dat2, VCV = VCV, model = mod) } # Helper function to add Mean (95% CI) labels dynamically one line above k-values in orchard plots add_ci_labels <- function(plot_obj, orchard_results) { est <- sprintf("%.2f", orchard_results$mod_table$estimate) ci_l <- sprintf("%.2f", orchard_results$mod_table$lowerCL) ci_u <- sprintf("%.2f", orchard_results$mod_table$upperCL) orig_labels <- plot_obj$layers[[6]]$aes_params$label new_labels <- c() for (i in 1:length(orig_labels)) { # Extract the original sample size numbers using regex nums <- regmatches(orig_labels[i], gregexpr("[0-9]+", orig_labels[i]))[[1]] if (length(nums) >= 2) { k_val <- nums[1] n_val <- nums[2] k_text <- paste0("k = ", k_val, " (", n_val, ")") } else { k_text <- orig_labels[i] } # Place Mean (95% CI) on the first line and k = xx (xx) on the second line new_labels[i] <- paste0(est[i], " [", ci_l[i], ", ", ci_u[i], "]\n", k_text) } plot_obj$layers[[6]]$aes_params$label <- new_labels plot_obj$layers[[6]]$geom_params$parse <- FALSE plot_obj$layers[[6]]$aes_params$hjust <- "right" plot_obj$layers[[6]]$aes_params$vjust <- 0.5 plot_obj$layers[[6]]$aes_params$size <- 3.0 # Slightly smaller font size to fit better plot_obj } ``` ::: --- ## Multi-Level Synthesis Results We fit the multilevel meta-analysis across all three database splits (New Only, Original Only, and Combined) for both effect size classes. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # HEDGES' G MODELS (UNCORRECTED) — now incorporating phylogenetic relatedness # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # New-only uses the new-extraction tree; Original-only and Combined use the # combined Hedges' g tree (Davies species are a subset of the combined tree). res_hedges_new <- fit_overall_mv(db_hedges %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "New Extraction (Santos)"), phylo_cor = phylo_cor_new) res_hedges_orig <- fit_overall_mv(db_hedges %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "Original (Davies 2020)"), phylo_cor = phylo_cor_hedges) res_hedges_comb <- fit_overall_mv(db_hedges, phylo_cor = phylo_cor_hedges) # HEDGES' G MODELS (BIAS-CORRECTED) res_hedges_new_corr <- fit_corrected_mv(db_hedges %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "New Extraction (Santos)"), moderator = "vi") res_hedges_orig_corr <- fit_corrected_mv(db_hedges %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "Original (Davies 2020)"), moderator = "vi") res_hedges_comb_corr <- fit_corrected_mv(db_hedges, moderator = "vi") # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # ODDS RATIO MODELS (UNCORRECTED) — now incorporating phylogenetic relatedness # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # New-only uses the new-extraction tree; Original-only and Combined use the # combined log OR tree (Jones species are a subset of the combined tree). res_or_new <- fit_overall_mv(db_or %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "New Extraction (Santos)"), phylo_cor = phylo_cor_new) res_or_orig <- fit_overall_mv(db_or %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)"), phylo_cor = phylo_cor_or) res_or_comb <- fit_overall_mv(db_or, phylo_cor = phylo_cor_or) # ODDS RATIO MODELS (BIAS-CORRECTED) res_or_new_corr <- fit_corrected_mv(db_or %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "New Extraction (Santos)"), moderator = "vi") res_or_orig_corr <- fit_corrected_mv(db_or %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)"), moderator = "vi") res_or_comb_corr <- fit_corrected_mv(db_or, moderator = "vi") ``` ::: --- ## Results and Visualizations ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Hedges' $g$ (Continuous outcomes) ::: {.panel-tabset} #### Uncorrected Overall Effect This plot displays the standard multi-level meta-analytic summaries across the three splits (New Only, Original Only, and Combined) *without* adjusting for publication bias. Points represent individual effect sizes colored by their data source. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} if (!is.null(res_hedges_new) && !is.null(res_hedges_orig) && !is.null(res_hedges_comb)) { # 1. Extract S3 result objects using mod_results h_new <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_hedges_new$model, group = "identifierStudyId") h_orig <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_hedges_orig$model, group = "identifierStudyId") h_comb <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_hedges_comb$model, group = "identifierStudyId") # 2. Clone S3 result objects to lists for manual editing class(h_new) <- "list" h_new$mod_table$name <- "New Extraction (Santos)" h_new$data$moderator <- "New Extraction (Santos)" class(h_new) <- c("orchard", "data.frame") class(h_orig) <- "list" h_orig$mod_table$name <- "Original (Davies 2020)" h_orig$data$moderator <- "Original (Davies 2020)" class(h_orig) <- c("orchard", "data.frame") class(h_comb) <- "list" h_comb$mod_table$name <- "Combined" h_comb$data$moderator <- "Combined" class(h_comb) <- c("orchard", "data.frame") # 3. Merge model results sub_hedges <- orchaRd::submerge(h_new, h_orig, h_comb) # 4. Add point-level real source mapping class(sub_hedges) <- "list" sub_hedges$data$real_source <- c( rep("New Extraction (Santos)", nrow(h_new$data)), rep("Original (Davies 2020)", nrow(h_orig$data)), rep("New Extraction (Santos)", nrow(h_new$data)), rep("Original (Davies 2020)", nrow(h_orig$data)) ) class(sub_hedges) <- c("orchard", "data.frame") # 4. Generate Plot p_hedges <- orchaRd::orchard_plot(sub_hedges, xlab = "Hedges' g", flip = TRUE) # 5. Customize color mapping for points p_hedges$layers[[1]]$mapping$colour <- ggplot2::aes(colour = real_source)$colour p_hedges$layers[[1]]$mapping$fill <- ggplot2::aes(fill = real_source)$fill # 6. Apply themes & custom palette (colorblind friendly orange/blue) p_hedges <- p_hedges + ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Davies 2020)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") + ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Davies 2020)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") + ggplot2::ggtitle("Overall Synthesis — Hedges' g Estimates (Uncorrected)") + ggplot2::theme( legend.position = c(0.98, 0.02), legend.justification = c("right", "bottom"), legend.background = ggplot2::element_rect(fill = scales::alpha("white", 0.75), color = "grey90"), legend.box = "vertical", legend.margin = ggplot2::margin(t = 4, r = 4, b = 4, l = 4), legend.title = ggplot2::element_text(size = 9, face = "bold"), legend.text = ggplot2::element_text(size = 8) ) # Add Mean (95% CI) and k labels p_hedges <- add_ci_labels(p_hedges, sub_hedges) print(p_hedges) } ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](overall_effect_files/figure-html/hedges_unified_plot-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: #### Bias-Corrected Overall Effect This plot displays the small-study publication bias corrected meta-analytic summaries (evaluated at $\text{V} = 0$, representing the theoretical estimate for a study of infinite sample size/precision) using the two-step Egger-type meta-regression framework. Points represent the same raw effect sizes. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} if (!is.null(res_hedges_new_corr) && !is.null(res_hedges_orig_corr) && !is.null(res_hedges_comb_corr)) { # 1. Extract S3 result objects using mod_results at vi = 0 h_new_corr <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_hedges_new_corr$model, group = "identifierStudyId", at = list(vi = 0), mod = "1") h_orig_corr <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_hedges_orig_corr$model, group = "identifierStudyId", at = list(vi = 0), mod = "1") h_comb_corr <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_hedges_comb_corr$model, group = "identifierStudyId", at = list(vi = 0), mod = "1") # 2. To make the comparison easier to read, we produce **two figures** — one for Hedges' $g$ and one for the log odds ratio — using the same `orchaRd::pub_bias_plot()` approach as the publication-bias chapter. Each figure stacks the three model levels (New Extraction, Original, Combined) as rows; within each row a single orchard trunk shows the uncorrected multilevel mean drawn over its raw effect sizes **once**, with the two corrected means overlaid on top. The raw points are coloured by data source and the legend is boxed in the corner, matching the side-by-side comparison plots above. The three estimates shown per subplot are: * **Uncorrected** (the trunk) — the multilevel `rma.mv` mean. * **Bias-Corrected (Nakagawa et al. [-@nakagawaMethodsTestingPublication2022]); purple overlay** — the intercept of the multilevel meta-regression with sampling variance ($v_i$) as a moderator, evaluated at $v_i = 0$ (the effect a hypothetical infinitely-precise study would report). * **Bias-Robust (Yang et al. [-@yangRobustPointVariance2024]); green overlay** — a fixed-/common-effect model with cluster-robust (CR2) variance estimation clustered by study. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Yang et al. (2024) bias-robust estimate (fixed-effect + CR2 cluster-robust), # fitted for each database split. Mirrors the construction in publication_bias.qmd # but applied to New, Original, and Combined subsets. fit_bias_robust <- function(dat, yi = "yi", vi = "vi", cluster_var = "identifierStudyId") { dat2 <- dat %>% filter(!is.na(.data[[yi]]), !is.na(.data[[vi]]), .data[[vi]] > 0) if (nrow(dat2) == 0) return(NULL) tryCatch({ fe <- metafor::rma(yi = dat2[[yi]], vi = dat2[[vi]], method = "FE", test = "t") metafor::robust(fe, cluster = dat2[[cluster_var]], clubSandwich = TRUE) }, error = function(e) { warning("Bias-robust fit failed: ", conditionMessage(e)); NULL }) } rob_hedges_new <- fit_bias_robust(db_hedges %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "New Extraction (Santos)")) rob_hedges_orig <- fit_bias_robust(db_hedges %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "Original (Davies 2020)")) rob_hedges_comb <- fit_bias_robust(db_hedges) rob_or_new <- fit_bias_robust(db_or %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "New Extraction (Santos)")) rob_or_orig <- fit_bias_robust(db_or %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)")) rob_or_comb <- fit_bias_robust(db_or) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # Subplot builder: one orchard trunk per model level, styled to match the # `hedges_comparison_plot` aesthetics — raw points coloured by data source # (colourblind orange/blue) with the boxed corner legend. # # The two corrected means are overlaid by hand (rather than via # orchaRd::pub_bias_plot(), whose colours are hardcoded red/blue and whose text # sits on top of the data) so that we can: # * use overlay colours that stand out from the orange/blue raw points # (green = Bias-Robust / Yang 2024, purple = Bias-Corrected / Nakagawa 2022); # * right-align the estimate text on the far side of the panel, kept inside # the plot box; # * share a common x-axis (effect-size) range across the stacked panels. # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- source_palette <- c( "New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Davies 2020)" = "#0072B2", "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)" = "#0072B2" ) # Overlay colours — chosen to be distinct from the orange/blue raw points and # colourblind-distinguishable (Okabe-Ito green + a strong violet). yang_col <- "#009E73" # Bias-Robust (Yang et al. 2024) naka_col <- "#7B2D8E" # Bias-Corrected (Nakagawa et al. 2022) # Extract the corrected meta-analytic mean + 95% CI from an (intercept-only or # vi-moderated) rma / rma.mv model — same intercept used by orchaRd::get_ints_dat. corrected_est <- function(model) { b <- tryCatch(as.numeric(model$b["intrcpt", ]), error = function(e) as.numeric(model$b[1])) data.frame(name = "Intrcpt", pred = b, ci.lb = model$ci.lb[1], ci.ub = model$ci.ub[1]) } # src: either a single data-source label (recycled) or a per-point character # vector matching the order of the model's effect sizes (used for Combined). # xlim: optional c(lo, hi) shared effect-size range for axis alignment; when # supplied, the estimate text is right-aligned to xlim[2] (inside the box). make_pub_bias_subplot <- function(unc_res, corr_res, rob_model, src, xlab, title, xlim = NULL) { if (is.null(unc_res) || is.null(unc_res$model) || is.null(corr_res) || is.null(corr_res$model) || is.null(rob_model)) { return(NULL) } # Single-trunk orchard result; attach the true per-point data source. h <- orchaRd::mod_results(unc_res$model, group = "identifierStudyId") if (length(src) == 1) src <- rep(src, nrow(h$data)) class(h) <- "list" h$data$real_source <- src class(h) <- c("orchard", "data.frame") # k/g labels to the left so the right margin is free for the estimate text. # Widen point.size range so bubble area tracks precision more visibly. # trunk.size = 1 -> point estimate drawn at size 4 (matches the overlay diamonds). oc <- orchaRd::orchard_plot(h, xlab = xlab, flip = TRUE, k.pos = "left", point.size = c(0.8, 10), trunk.size = 1) # Recolour the raw points (layer 1) by data source — as in hedges_comparison_plot. oc$layers[[1]]$mapping$colour <- ggplot2::aes(colour = real_source)$colour oc$layers[[1]]$mapping$fill <- ggplot2::aes(fill = real_source)$fill # Corrected estimates to overlay on the single uncorrected trunk. yang <- corrected_est(rob_model) # Bias-Robust (Yang 2024) naka <- corrected_est(corr_res$model) # Bias-Corrected (Nakagawa 2022) # Common effect-size range (falls back to this panel's data if not supplied). if (is.null(xlim)) { rng <- range(c(h$data$yi, yang$ci.lb, yang$ci.ub, naka$ci.lb, naka$ci.ub), na.rm = TRUE, finite = TRUE) pad <- diff(rng) * 0.08 xlim <- c(rng[1] - pad, rng[2] + pad) } txt_y <- xlim[2] # right edge (effect-size axis is horizontal after flip) oc <- oc + # Bias-Robust (Yang) — nudged below the trunk. Black CI bar; coloured # diamond with a black border so it stands out against the bubbles. ggplot2::geom_linerange(data = yang, ggplot2::aes(x = name, ymin = ci.lb, ymax = ci.ub), colour = "black", linewidth = 1.2, position = ggplot2::position_nudge(x = -0.08), inherit.aes = FALSE) + ggplot2::geom_point(data = yang, ggplot2::aes(x = name, y = pred), fill = yang_col, colour = "black", shape = 23, size = 4, stroke = 0.9, position = ggplot2::position_nudge(x = -0.08), inherit.aes = FALSE) + # Bias-Corrected (Nakagawa) — nudged above the trunk. Black CI bar; # coloured diamond with a black border. ggplot2::geom_linerange(data = naka, ggplot2::aes(x = name, ymin = ci.lb, ymax = ci.ub), colour = "black", linewidth = 1.2, position = ggplot2::position_nudge(x = 0.08), inherit.aes = FALSE) + ggplot2::geom_point(data = naka, ggplot2::aes(x = name, y = pred), fill = naka_col, colour = "black", shape = 23, size = 4, stroke = 0.9, position = ggplot2::position_nudge(x = 0.08), inherit.aes = FALSE) + # Estimate text, right-aligned to the right edge so it stays in the box. # x positions mirror each diamond's nudge: Yang below (x<1), Naka above (x>1). ggplot2::annotate("text", x = 0.66, y = txt_y, hjust = 1, vjust = 0.5, colour = yang_col, size = 3.4, fontface = "bold", label = sprintf("Bias-Robust (Yang): %.2f [%.2f, %.2f]", yang$pred, yang$ci.lb, yang$ci.ub)) + ggplot2::annotate("text", x = 1.34, y = txt_y, hjust = 1, vjust = 0.5, colour = naka_col, size = 3.4, fontface = "bold", label = sprintf("Bias-Corrected (Nakagawa): %.2f [%.2f, %.2f]", naka$pred, naka$ci.lb, naka$ci.ub)) + ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(values = source_palette, name = "Data Source") + ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = source_palette, name = "Data Source") + ggplot2::ggtitle(title) + ggplot2::coord_flip(ylim = xlim) + ggplot2::theme( legend.position = c(0.98, 0.02), legend.justification = c("right", "bottom"), legend.background = ggplot2::element_rect(fill = scales::alpha("white", 0.75), color = "grey90"), legend.box = "vertical", legend.margin = ggplot2::margin(t = 4, r = 4, b = 4, l = 4), legend.title = ggplot2::element_text(size = 9, face = "bold"), legend.text = ggplot2::element_text(size = 8), plot.title = ggplot2::element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5), # Panel identifier (a)/(b)/(c) in the top-left corner of the box. plot.tag = ggplot2::element_text(size = 13, face = "bold", hjust = 0, vjust = 1), plot.tag.position = c(0.015, 0.985) ) oc } # Shared effect-size range across the three panels of a stack, padded for the # right-aligned estimate text. Includes raw points + all corrected-estimate CIs. compute_stack_xlim <- function(db, corr_models, rob_models) { ci_vals <- function(m) if (is.null(m)) numeric(0) else c(m$ci.lb[1], m$ci.ub[1]) vals <- c(db$yi, unlist(lapply(corr_models, function(r) if (is.null(r)) numeric(0) else ci_vals(r$model))), unlist(lapply(rob_models, ci_vals))) vals <- vals[is.finite(vals)] rng <- range(vals) pad <- diff(rng) * 0.08 c(rng[1] - pad, rng[2] + pad) } # Per-point source vectors for the Combined trunks (new rows then original rows, # matching bind_rows() order — same assumption as hedges_comparison_plot). n_h_new <- nrow(orchaRd::mod_results(res_hedges_new$model, group = "identifierStudyId")$data) n_h_orig <- nrow(orchaRd::mod_results(res_hedges_orig$model, group = "identifierStudyId")$data) src_h_comb <- c(rep("New Extraction (Santos)", n_h_new), rep("Original (Davies 2020)", n_h_orig)) n_o_new <- nrow(orchaRd::mod_results(res_or_new$model, group = "identifierStudyId")$data) n_o_orig <- nrow(orchaRd::mod_results(res_or_orig$model, group = "identifierStudyId")$data) src_o_comb <- c(rep("New Extraction (Santos)", n_o_new), rep("Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)", n_o_orig)) ``` ::: ### Hedges' $g$ (Continuous outcomes) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Shared x-axis range so the three Hedges panels align for easy comparison. hedges_xlim <- compute_stack_xlim( db_hedges, list(res_hedges_new_corr, res_hedges_orig_corr, res_hedges_comb_corr), list(rob_hedges_new, rob_hedges_orig, rob_hedges_comb) ) ph_new <- make_pub_bias_subplot(res_hedges_new, res_hedges_new_corr, rob_hedges_new, "New Extraction (Santos)", "Hedges' g", "New Extraction", xlim = hedges_xlim) ph_orig <- make_pub_bias_subplot(res_hedges_orig, res_hedges_orig_corr, rob_hedges_orig, "Original (Davies 2020)", "Hedges' g", "Original", xlim = hedges_xlim) ph_comb <- make_pub_bias_subplot(res_hedges_comb, res_hedges_comb_corr, rob_hedges_comb, src_h_comb, "Hedges' g", "Combined", xlim = hedges_xlim) fig_hedges <- (ph_orig / ph_new / ph_comb) + patchwork::plot_annotation( tag_levels = "a", tag_prefix = "(", tag_suffix = ")", title = "Hedges' g — Uncorrected mean with Bias-Corrected & Bias-Robust overlays", subtitle = "Overlays: purple = Bias-Corrected (Nakagawa 2022), green = Bias-Robust (Yang 2024).", theme = ggplot2::theme( plot.title = ggplot2::element_text(size = 15, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5), plot.subtitle = ggplot2::element_text(size = 11, face = "italic", hjust = 0.5) ) ) print(fig_hedges) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](overall_effect_files/figure-html/correction_comparison_hedges-1.png){width=864} ::: ::: ### Odds Ratio (Binary outcomes) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Shared x-axis range so the three OR panels align for easy comparison. or_xlim <- compute_stack_xlim( db_or, list(res_or_new_corr, res_or_orig_corr, res_or_comb_corr), list(rob_or_new, rob_or_orig, rob_or_comb) ) po_new <- make_pub_bias_subplot(res_or_new, res_or_new_corr, rob_or_new, "New Extraction (Santos)", "Log Odds Ratio (lnOR)", "New Extraction", xlim = or_xlim) po_orig <- make_pub_bias_subplot(res_or_orig, res_or_orig_corr, rob_or_orig, "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)", "Log Odds Ratio (lnOR)", "Original", xlim = or_xlim) po_comb <- make_pub_bias_subplot(res_or_comb, res_or_comb_corr, rob_or_comb, src_o_comb, "Log Odds Ratio (lnOR)", "Combined", xlim = or_xlim) fig_or <- (po_orig / po_new / po_comb) + patchwork::plot_annotation( tag_levels = "a", tag_prefix = "(", tag_suffix = ")", title = "Log Odds Ratio — Uncorrected mean with Bias-Corrected & Bias-Robust overlays", subtitle = "Overlays: purple = Bias-Corrected (Nakagawa 2022), green = Bias-Robust (Yang 2024).", theme = ggplot2::theme( plot.title = ggplot2::element_text(size = 15, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5), plot.subtitle = ggplot2::element_text(size = 11, face = "italic", hjust = 0.5) ) ) print(fig_or) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](overall_effect_files/figure-html/correction_comparison_or-1.png){width=864} ::: ::: --- ## Taxonomic Moderator (Heteroscedastic Model) {#sec-taxonomic-moderator} The combined synthesis pools effect sizes across very different organisms — insects and other arthropods, ray-finned fishes, and the remaining vertebrates (birds and mammals). Two questions follow naturally: does the *average* strength of mate-choice copying differ among these groups, and is the *amount of heterogeneity* itself group-specific? To answer both we fit a single `rma.mv` model with **taxonomic group as a categorical moderator** and a **heteroscedastic** random-effects structure that lets the variance components differ across groups. Three design choices define this model: * **Taxonomic grouping.** Each species (full binomial) is assigned to **Arthropods**, **Fish**, or **Other Vertebrates** by genus. The genus uniquely determines the class for every species in these data, so the mapping is unambiguous (verified in the table below). * **No phylogenetic term.** The heterogeneity decomposition shows the phylogenetic component contributes very little variance, so it is dropped here; the species random intercept (`taxonomySpecies`) still absorbs among-species differences within each group. * **Heteroscedasticity (`struct = "HCS"`, `rho = 0`).** The **residual (within-study) effect-size** variance is allowed to take a separate value in each taxonomic group, over a single pooled between-study variance, rather than forcing one common residual variance. This follows the `orchaRd` 2.0 recommendation for meta-regression with heterogeneous variances [@nakagawaOrchaRd2Visualising2023]: the heteroscedastic random term is written as an `~ moderator | unit` formula (`~ tax_group | identifierEffectSizeID`) with a heteroscedastic compound-symmetry structure and the between-level covariance fixed to zero (`rho = 0`), since each effect size belongs to exactly one taxonomic group and a cross-group covariance is not identifiable. (A fuller structure that *also* made the between-study variance group-specific was considered but dropped: its per-group between-study variances collapse to zero for the groups with few studies, so it is over-parameterised for these data.) * **Marginalised means.** The moderator is fitted **with an intercept** (`mods = ~ tax_group`) and the per-group means, confidence intervals, and prediction intervals are obtained as **marginalised means** through `orchaRd::mod_results()` (which calls `emmeans` internally), again as recommended in the `orchaRd` 2.0 workflow. The overall test for differences among groups is the model's omnibus moderator test ($Q_M$). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # Genus-based taxonomic classification of the combined-dataset species. # Classification keys on the FULL binomial (`species_phylo`); the genus alone # unambiguously determines the class for every species in these datasets # (cross-checked against the higher-taxon cascade in build_phylogeny.R). # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- arthropod_genera <- c("Drosophila", "Enchenopa", "Schizocosa", "Uca") fish_genera <- c( "Amblyglyphidodon", "Brachyrhaphis", "Danio", "Etheostoma", "Gambusia", "Gasterosteus", "Limia", "Oryzias", "Poecilia", "Pomatoschistus", "Porichthys", "Rhabdoblennius", "Syngnathus" ) othervert_genera <- c("Coturnix", "Dama", "Ficedula", "Molothrus", "Mus", "Rattus", "Taeniopygia") tax_levels <- c("Arthropods", "Fish", "Other Vertebrates") tax_palette <- c("Arthropods" = "#E69F00", "Fish" = "#56B4E9", "Other Vertebrates" = "#009E73") classify_taxon <- function(binomial) { genus <- stringr::word(as.character(binomial), 1) grp <- dplyr::case_when( genus %in% arthropod_genera ~ "Arthropods", genus %in% fish_genera ~ "Fish", genus %in% othervert_genera ~ "Other Vertebrates", TRUE ~ NA_character_ ) factor(grp, levels = tax_levels) } # Add the taxonomic group to the combined Hedges' g and log OR datasets. db_hedges_tax <- db_hedges %>% mutate(tax_group = classify_taxon(species_phylo)) db_or_tax <- db_or %>% mutate(tax_group = classify_taxon(species_phylo)) # Defensive check: every species should be classified. unmapped_tax <- sort(unique(as.character(c( db_hedges_tax$species_phylo[is.na(db_hedges_tax$tax_group)], db_or_tax$species_phylo[is.na(db_or_tax$tax_group)] )))) if (length(unmapped_tax) > 0) { warning("Unclassified species (no taxonomic group): ", paste(unmapped_tax, collapse = ", ")) } ``` ::: ### Species-to-Group Mapping (verification) The table below lists every species in the combined datasets, its full binomial, the species level used as a random effect (the epithet), and the taxonomic group it was assigned to, with the number of effect sizes it contributes to each effect-size class. This is the audit the analysis is built on — each row should look correct. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Model Fitting ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # Heteroscedastic taxonomic-moderator model, following the orchaRd 2.0 # heterogeneous-variance workflow (Nakagawa et al. 2023, vignette Example 4). # # * The moderator is fitted WITH an intercept (`mods = ~ tax_group`); per-group # means / CIs / PIs are obtained downstream as marginalised means via # orchaRd::mod_results() (emmeans), and the among-group test is the model's # omnibus moderator test (QM). # * The heterogeneous (residual) variance is specified as a `~ tax_group | unit` # random term with struct = "HCS" and the between-level covariance fixed to # zero (rho = 0) — exactly the structure used in the orchaRd documentation. # With each unit belonging to a single group this estimates one residual # variance per group with no cross-group covariance. # * PRIMARY structure: group-specific RESIDUAL variance # (`~ tax_group | identifierEffectSizeID`) over a single pooled between-study # variance (`~ 1 | identifierStudyId`) and a homoscedastic species intercept # (`~ 1 | taxonomySpecies`). NO phylogenetic term. (The fuller both-level # structure was dropped: its per-group between-study variances collapse to # zero for the thin groups, i.e. it is over-parameterised for these data.) # * Each heteroscedastic structure is tried with several optimisers (nlminb, # optim/BFGS, optim/L-BFGS-B, then a high-iteration Nelder-Mead) before being # declared a failure; only if none converge does the cascade fall back to a # simpler structure. The vignette's default Nelder-Mead alone hits its # iteration cap on these data (convergence = 1), so robust optimisers go first. # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- fit_taxon_hetero_mv <- function(dat, group_var = "tax_group", yi = "yi", vi = "vi", cluster_var = "identifierStudyId", obs_var = "identifierEffectSizeID", rho = 0.5) { dat2 <- dat %>% filter(!is.na(.data[[yi]]), !is.na(.data[[vi]]), .data[[vi]] > 0, !is.na(.data[[group_var]])) if (nrow(dat2) == 0) { message("No complete cases found.") return(NULL) } dat2[[group_var]] <- droplevels(factor(dat2[[group_var]], levels = tax_levels)) VCV <- metafor::vcalc(vi = dat2[[vi]], cluster = dat2[[cluster_var]], obs = dat2[[obs_var]], rho = rho, data = dat2) mods_f <- stats::as.formula(paste0("~ ", group_var)) # intercept-coded obs_f <- stats::as.formula(paste0("~ ", group_var, " | ", obs_var)) # heteroscedastic residual # Optimisers tried (in order) for the heteroscedastic structures. The default # Nelder-Mead used in the orchaRd vignette hits its iteration cap on these # data (convergence = 1), so we lead with the more robust derivative-based # optimisers and only then fall back to a high-iteration Nelder-Mead. opt_grid <- list( "nlminb" = list(optimizer = "nlminb", iter.max = 10000, eval.max = 10000), "optim/BFGS" = list(optimizer = "optim", optmethod = "BFGS", maxit = 10000), "optim/L-BFGS-B" = list(optimizer = "optim", optmethod = "L-BFGS-B", maxit = 10000), "optim/Nelder-Mead" = list(optimizer = "optim", optmethod = "Nelder-Mead", maxit = 50000, reltol = 1e-9) ) # Build one rma.mv call for a candidate structure + optimiser control. build <- function(cand, ctrl) { args <- c( list(yi = dat2[[yi]], V = VCV, mods = mods_f, random = cand$random, test = "t", dfs = "contain", method = "REML", sparse = TRUE, data = dat2), cand$args ) if (!is.null(cand$struct)) args$struct <- cand$struct if (!is.null(ctrl)) args$control <- ctrl do.call(metafor::rma.mv, args) } # Candidate structures, tried in order. The cascade stops at the FIRST one # that fits without error; warnings do NOT stop the cascade but are recorded. # PRIMARY = residual-only HCS (orchaRd Example 4 default): a single pooled # between-study variance plus a group-specific residual variance. The fuller # both-level structure was dropped because its per-group between-study # variances collapse to the zero boundary for the thin groups (Other # Vertebrates, Arthropods) — i.e. it is over-parameterised for these data. candidates <- list( list(spec = "Heteroscedastic (residual only, per group; HCS, rho = 0)", random = list(~ 1 | identifierStudyId, obs_f, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies), struct = "HCS", args = list(rho = 0), het = TRUE), list(spec = "Homoscedastic (single variance per component)", random = list(~ 1 | identifierStudyId, ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies), struct = NULL, args = list(), het = FALSE) ) # Attempt one candidate. Heteroscedastic structures cycle through opt_grid # until one converges; the homoscedastic structure uses the metafor default. # Errors AND warnings are captured (warnings do not abort). fit_once <- function(cand, ctrl) { warns <- character(0) t0 <- Sys.time() mod <- tryCatch( withCallingHandlers( build(cand, ctrl), warning = function(w) { warns[[length(warns) + 1L]] <<- conditionMessage(w) invokeRestart("muffleWarning") } ), error = function(e) structure(list(msg = conditionMessage(e)), class = "tax_fit_error") ) list(mod = mod, warns = warns, secs = as.numeric(difftime(Sys.time(), t0, units = "secs"))) } attempt <- function(cand) { if (!isTRUE(cand$het)) { r <- fit_once(cand, NULL) failed <- inherits(r$mod, "tax_fit_error") return(list(model = if (failed) NULL else r$mod, failed = failed, error = if (failed) r$mod$msg else NA_character_, warnings = r$warns, secs = r$secs, optimizer = "default (nlminb)")) } msgs <- character(0) total <- 0 for (onm in names(opt_grid)) { r <- fit_once(cand, opt_grid[[onm]]) total <- total + r$secs if (!inherits(r$mod, "tax_fit_error")) { return(list(model = r$mod, failed = FALSE, error = NA_character_, warnings = r$warns, secs = total, optimizer = onm)) } msgs <- c(msgs, paste0(onm, ": ", r$mod$msg)) } list(model = NULL, failed = TRUE, error = paste(msgs, collapse = " || "), warnings = character(0), secs = total, optimizer = NA_character_) } diag_rows <- list() chosen <- NULL chosen_spec <- NA_character_ for (i in seq_along(candidates)) { cand <- candidates[[i]] if (!is.null(chosen)) { diag_rows[[i]] <- tibble::tibble( stage = i, structure = cand$spec, status = "not attempted", optimizer = NA_character_, seconds = NA_real_, n_warnings = NA_integer_, detail = "an earlier structure succeeded" ) next } a <- attempt(cand) status <- if (a$failed) "ERROR" else if (length(a$warnings) > 0) "OK (with warnings)" else "OK" detail <- if (a$failed) a$error else if (length(a$warnings) > 0) paste(a$warnings, collapse = " || ") else "clean fit" diag_rows[[i]] <- tibble::tibble( stage = i, structure = cand$spec, status = status, optimizer = a$optimizer, seconds = round(a$secs, 2), n_warnings = length(a$warnings), detail = detail ) if (!a$failed) { chosen <- a$model chosen_spec <- cand$spec } } diagnostics <- dplyr::bind_rows(diag_rows) if (is.null(chosen)) { warning("All taxonomic-moderator structures failed to fit.") return(list(data = dat2, VCV = VCV, model = NULL, spec = "ALL FAILED", diagnostics = diagnostics)) } list(data = dat2, VCV = VCV, model = chosen, spec = chosen_spec, diagnostics = diagnostics) } res_tax_hedges <- fit_taxon_hetero_mv(db_hedges_tax) res_tax_or <- fit_taxon_hetero_mv(db_or_tax) # Omnibus among-group test. With the intercept-coded moderator, metafor's built-in # QM tests all non-intercept coefficients jointly — i.e. exactly whether the group # means differ — so it is used directly (no custom contrast matrix needed). among_group_test <- function(res) { if (is.null(res) || is.null(res$model)) { return(NULL) } list(QM = res$model$QM, QMp = res$model$QMp, QMdf = res$model$QMdf) } qm_tax_hedges <- among_group_test(res_tax_hedges) qm_tax_or <- among_group_test(res_tax_or) # Total I^2 (orchaRd if available; otherwise a Higgins typical-variance fallback # so a heterogeneity figure is always reported even under the heteroscedastic # structure). total_i2 <- function(res) { if (is.null(res) || is.null(res$model)) { return(NA_real_) } m <- res$model i2 <- tryCatch(orchaRd::i2_ml(m), error = function(e) NULL) if (!is.null(i2) && any(grepl("Total", names(i2)))) { return(as.numeric(i2[grep("Total", names(i2))[1]])) } vi <- res$data$vi wi <- 1 / vi k <- length(vi) s2 <- (k - 1) * sum(wi) / (sum(wi)^2 - sum(wi^2)) vc <- sum(c(m$sigma2, m$tau2, m$gamma2), na.rm = TRUE) 100 * vc / (vc + s2) } i2_tax_hedges <- total_i2(res_tax_hedges) i2_tax_or <- total_i2(res_tax_or) # Persist the fitted results + cascade diagnostics so they can be inspected # interactively in an R session WITHOUT re-rendering the book, e.g.: # b <- readRDS("outputs/1_effect_size_calculation_pipeline/taxon_model_fits.rds") # b$hedges$spec ; b$hedges$diagnostics ; summary(b$hedges$model) # (also consumed by scripts/1_effect_size_calculation_pipeline/diagnose_taxon_model.R) tryCatch({ tax_out_dir <- here::here("outputs", "1_effect_size_calculation_pipeline") dir.create(tax_out_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE) saveRDS( list( hedges = res_tax_hedges, or = res_tax_or, qm = list(hedges = qm_tax_hedges, or = qm_tax_or), i2 = list(hedges = i2_tax_hedges, or = i2_tax_or), saved_at = Sys.time() ), file.path(tax_out_dir, "taxon_model_fits.rds") ) }, error = function(e) warning("Could not save taxon_model_fits.rds: ", conditionMessage(e))) ``` ::: ### Fit Diagnostics {#sec-taxon-fit-diagnostics} The model is fitted through a **fallback cascade**: the most flexible heteroscedastic structure is attempted first, and only if it *errors* does the next-simpler structure get tried (warnings do **not** trigger a fallback, but are captured and shown). In a multilevel model this total can be *partitioned* into the contribution of each random effect [@nakagawaMethodsTestingPublication2022]. Here the variance is split among four components: between-**study** differences, the residual **effect-size** (within-study) level, a **species** (non-phylogenetic) component, and a **phylogenetic** component that captures variance shared among species in proportion to their relatedness. We compute these partial $I^2$ values with `orchaRd::i2_ml()` for each uncorrected model. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Human-readable labels for the i2_ml components (names are prefixed with "I2_") comp_labels <- c( Total = "Total", identifierStudyId = "Study", identifierEffectSizeID = "Effect size (residual)", taxonomySpecies = "Species (non-phylogenetic)", species_phylo = "Phylogeny" ) # Pull the partial I^2 vector from a fitted model into tidy long form get_i2 <- function(res, effect, model) { if (is.null(res) || is.null(res$model)) { return(NULL) } v <- tryCatch(orchaRd::i2_ml(res$model), error = function(e) NULL) if (is.null(v)) { return(NULL) } tibble::tibble( Effect = effect, Model = model, Component = sub("^I2_", "", names(v)), I2 = as.numeric(v) ) } i2_long <- dplyr::bind_rows( get_i2(res_hedges_new, "Hedges' g", "New Only"), get_i2(res_hedges_orig, "Hedges' g", "Original Only"), get_i2(res_hedges_comb, "Hedges' g", "Combined"), get_i2(res_or_new, "Log OR", "New Only"), get_i2(res_or_orig, "Log OR", "Original Only"), get_i2(res_or_comb, "Log OR", "Combined") ) %>% mutate(ComponentLabel = dplyr::recode(Component, !!!comp_labels)) # Lookup helper for inline narrative (returns NA if a component is absent) i2get <- function(effect, model, component) { v <- i2_long$I2[i2_long$Effect == effect & i2_long$Model == model & i2_long$ComponentLabel == component] if (length(v) == 0 || all(is.na(v))) NA_real_ else v[1] } ``` ::: ### Partial $I^2$ by Random Effect ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Interpreting the Heterogeneity {#sec-heterogeneity-narrative} The combined models show that mate-choice copying effect sizes are **highly heterogeneous**: total $I^2$ reaches 85.7% for Hedges' $g$ and 74.7% for the log odds ratio. In other words, the large majority of the observed variation reflects genuine differences among effects rather than sampling error alone, so the pooled means should be read as averages over a broad distribution of true effects rather than as a single common effect. Decomposing that heterogeneity is where the phylogeny becomes informative. For the combined Hedges' $g$ model, the **phylogenetic** component accounts for 3.5% of the variance, the **non-phylogenetic species** component for 3.1%, between-**study** differences for 7.4%, and the residual **effect-size** level for 71.7%. The combined log-odds-ratio model partitions similarly: phylogeny 1.1%, species 23.9%, study 4.3%, and residual 45.4%. The size of the phylogenetic component is the key new result. A substantial value would indicate that closely related species tend to show similar degrees of mate-choice copying — i.e., the trait carries a phylogenetic signal and effects are not independent across the tree — which would caution against treating species as exchangeable and would motivate phylogenetically informed interpretation. A small phylogenetic component, by contrast, indicates that relatedness explains little of the among-species variation: most of the species-level differences are idiosyncratic (captured by the non-phylogenetic species term) rather than inherited, and the earlier non-phylogenetic models were not materially biased by ignoring the tree. The contrast between the phylogenetic and non-phylogenetic species components is therefore the most direct readout of how much shared ancestry, as opposed to species-specific biology, structures the data. Two further patterns are worth noting. First, the residual effect-size level typically absorbs a large share of the variance, reflecting heterogeneity among individual trials within studies (different paradigms, demonstrators, and conditions). Second, comparing the New-Only, Original-Only, and Combined columns shows whether the new extraction introduces or dilutes heterogeneity relative to the legacy datasets; where the new and original subsets allocate variance differently, the combined estimate reflects a weighted blend of the two. Because this high heterogeneity is the rule rather than the exception, it justifies the moderator and sub-group analyses pursued elsewhere in the workflow, and it is the reason the pooled estimates are reported with prediction intervals in addition to confidence intervals. --- ## Sensitivity Analysis: Impact of the VCV Structure To evaluate the robustness of our meta-analytic results, we conduct a sensitivity analysis comparing the main multilevel models (which incorporate a Variance-Covariance [VCV] matrix with an assumed within-study correlation $\rho = 0.5$) against simplified multilevel models that ignore this correlation (i.e., treating effect size variances as independent diagonal elements, $V = v_i$). Ignoring within-study correlation can lead to improper weighting of studies with multiple effect sizes and can bias the standard errors and confidence intervals of the pooled estimates. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # 1. Define comparison function compare_vcv_models <- function(dat, name, yi = "yi", vi = "vi", cluster_var = "identifierStudyId", obs_var = "identifierEffectSizeID", rho = 0.5) { dat2 <- dat %>% filter(!is.na(.data[[yi]]), !is.na(.data[[vi]]), .data[[vi]] > 0) if (nrow(dat2) == 0) { return(NULL) } # A. With VCV (rho = 0.5) VCV <- metafor::vcalc( vi = dat2[[vi]], cluster = dat2[[cluster_var]], obs = dat2[[obs_var]], rho = rho, data = dat2 ) mod_w_vcv <- metafor::rma.mv( yi = dat2[[yi]], V = VCV, random = list( ~ 1 | identifierStudyId, ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies ), test = "t", method = "REML", sparse = TRUE, data = dat2 ) # B. Without VCV (diagonal V = vi) mod_no_vcv <- metafor::rma.mv( yi = dat2[[yi]], V = dat2[[vi]], random = list( ~ 1 | identifierStudyId, ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies ), test = "t", method = "REML", sparse = TRUE, data = dat2 ) # Combine results data.frame( Analysis = name, Model_Type = c("With VCV (rho = 0.5)", "Without VCV (Independent)"), k = c(mod_w_vcv$k, mod_no_vcv$k), Estimate = c(mod_w_vcv$b[1], mod_no_vcv$b[1]), SE = c(mod_w_vcv$se[1], mod_no_vcv$se[1]), CI_Lower = c(mod_w_vcv$ci.lb[1], mod_no_vcv$ci.lb[1]), CI_Upper = c(mod_w_vcv$ci.ub[1], mod_no_vcv$ci.ub[1]), p_val = c(mod_w_vcv$pval[1], mod_no_vcv$pval[1]) ) } # 2. Run comparisons across all subsets comp_hedges_new <- compare_vcv_models(db_hedges %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "New Extraction (Santos)"), "Hedges' g (New Only)") comp_hedges_orig <- compare_vcv_models(db_hedges %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "Original (Davies 2020)"), "Hedges' g (Original Only)") comp_hedges_comb <- compare_vcv_models(db_hedges, "Hedges' g (Combined)") comp_or_new <- compare_vcv_models(db_or %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "New Extraction (Santos)"), "Log OR (New Only)") comp_or_orig <- compare_vcv_models(db_or %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)"), "Log OR (Original Only)") comp_or_comb <- compare_vcv_models(db_or, "Log OR (Combined)") # 3. Bind all comparisons together vcv_comparison_results <- bind_rows( comp_hedges_new, comp_hedges_orig, comp_hedges_comb, comp_or_new, comp_or_orig, comp_or_comb ) ``` ::: ### Sensitivity Comparison Table The interactive table below compares the pool estimate, standard error (SE), 95% Confidence Intervals, and p-values for both models across all subsets. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Key Methodological Findings 1. **Marginal Shifts in Pooled Estimates:** Treating effect sizes as completely independent (Without VCV) causes very minor changes to the overall pooled estimates. For the combined dataset, the Hedges' $g$ estimate shifts from $0.46$ to $0.48$, while the Log Odds Ratio (lnOR) estimate shifts from $0.60$ to $0.64$. This indicates that the main directional conclusions of the meta-analyses are robust to VCV specification. 2. **Standard Error Shifts:** In this multilevel model specification (which partitions variance using nested random intercepts), omitting the VCV matrix and assuming independence leads to a *slight inflation* of the standard error and wider confidence intervals for the combined analyses: * **Hedges' $g$ (Combined):** SE increases from $0.07$ to $0.07$ (CI width increases from $0.29$ to $0.30$). * **Log Odds Ratio (Combined):** SE increases from $0.14$ to $0.15$ (CI width increases from $0.54$ to $0.59$). This demonstrates that accounting for known within-study covariance via `vcalc` yields more precise, correctly-weighted estimates. 3. **Impact on Statistical Significance:** For continuous outcomes (Hedges' $g$ Combined), the pooled estimate is statistically significant with the VCV matrix ($p < 0.0001$) and statistically significant without it ($p < 0.0001$). Binary outcomes (Log OR Combined) are statistically significant under both specifications ($p < 0.0001$ with VCV; $p < 0.0001$ without).
